patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] Documentation: remove the requirement for LLM attribution
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the requirement for attributing Large Language Model (LLM) assistance in kernel documentation, aiming to clarify the distinction between meaningful and trivial LLM contributions.

review_comments.author: SJ Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park noted that the 'Assisted-by: LLM' tag should only be used when the user has completely reviewed and understands the text, and suggested that patches without this level of review should not be submitted even if tagged.

Reviewer expressed skepticism about the difficulty of defining what constitutes 'Assisted-by: LLM' attribution, citing a series of hypothetical scenarios to illustrate the ambiguity
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this doesn't need the tag, too.  Like patches, the user should
completely reviewed and understand the text, though.  Without the complete
review, it should not be submmitted at all, regardless of the tag.

---

I'm not very sure...  I don't really feel the question is that difficult and
ambiguous to answer.  I may be biased.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug
[PATCH v2] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a memory hotplug issue where the allocation of per-cpu node statistics fails, leading to a NULL dereference when trying to access them. The patch propagates the -ENOMEM error up the stack and leaves per_cpu_nodestats pointing at boot_nodestats, allowing later online attempts to retry the allocation.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and gave an Acked-by tag.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Applied the patch to the fixes branch of the memblock.git tree.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 01 Jul 2026 18:16:13 -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> We miss a failed allocation check for pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, which
> results in a NULL deref when we offset into the per-cpu area.
> 
> Propagate -ENOMEM up the stack and leave per_cpu_nodestats pointing
> at boot_nodestats so a later online can retry the allocation.
> 
> hotadd_init_pgdat() returns NULL on failure, which __try_online_node()
> already maps to -ENOMEM.
> 
> [...]

Applied to fixes branch of memblock.git tree, thanks!

[1/1] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug
      commit: 2ebce860bdd7ae5e13002811bc9bbbf33fcfc221

tree: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/rppt/memblock
branch: fixes

--
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

review_comments.author: Laurent Pinchart
review_comments.summary: Laurent Pinchart noted that even if the Assisted-by tag is removed, it is still necessary to mention the requirement for disclosing usage of code-generation tools in Documentation/process/generated-content.rst.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> is quite low.
> 
> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> 
> 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.
> 
> 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> 
> Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
> Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> reliable.
> ---
>  Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst  | 22 ----------------------
>  Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst | 10 ----------

You're missing scripts/checkpatch.pl.

>  2 files changed, 32 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst b/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> index 899f4459c52d..c4cc0917fc92 100644
> --- a/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> @@ -35,25 +35,3 @@ is responsible for:
>  * Ensuring compliance with licensing requirements
>  * Adding their own Signed-off-by tag to certify the DCO
>  * Taking full responsibility for the contribution
> -
> -Attribution
> -===========
> -
> -When AI tools contribute to kernel development, proper attribution
> -helps track the evolving role of AI in the development process.
> -Contributions should include an Assisted-by tag in the following format::
> -
> -  Assisted-by: AGENT_NAME:MODEL_VERSION [TOOL1] [TOOL2]
> -
> -Where:
> -
> -* ``AGENT_NAME`` is the name of the AI tool or framework
> -* ``MODEL_VERSION`` is the specific model version used
> -* ``[TOOL1] [TOOL2]`` are optional specialized analysis tools used
> -  (e.g., coccinelle, sparse, smatch, clang-tidy)
> -
> -Basic development tools (git, gcc, make, editors) should not be listed.
> -
> -Example::
> -
> -  Assisted-by: Claude:claude-3-opus coccinelle sparse

Documentation/process/generated-content.rst still clearly requests
contributors to disclose usage of code-generation tools. Even if we drop
the Assisted-by tag, I think it is useful to mention the requirement
here (probably including a link to generated-content.rst).

> diff --git a/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst b/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst
> index cc6a1f73d7f2..b74c38aa9770 100644
> --- a/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst
> @@ -634,16 +634,6 @@ bugzilla.kernel.org is a public place in this sense, but email addresses
>  used there are private; so do not expose them in tags, unless the person
>  used them in earlier contributions.
>  
> -Using Assisted-by:
> -------------------
> -
> -If you used any sort of advanced coding tool in the creation of your patch,
> -you need to acknowledge that use by adding an Assisted-by tag.  Failure to
> -do so may impede the acceptance of your work.  Please see
> -Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst for details regarding the
> -acknowledgment of coding assistants.
> -
> -
>  .. _the_canonical_patch_format:
>  
>  The canonical patch format
> 
> ---
> base-commit: 665159e246749578d4e4bfe106ee3b74edcdab18
> change-id: 20260702-aidoc-7e18f221d63a

-- 
Regards,

Laurent Pinchart

---

Hi Lorenzo,

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 03:57:11PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> I'm a little surprised I'm cc'd on this :) I'm not entirely sure if my pushing
> back on this is going to mean anything but I suppose here goes nothing.
> 
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> > months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> > is quite low.
> >
> > 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> > the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> 
> Does something not being universally followed therefore make it worthless?
> 
> You really have to explain that, because this is literally true of any rule
> whatsoever we might have in the kernel, should we drop all of them?
> 
> I think you should replace this with a cogent argument such as that you feel it
> is not being used in the _majority_ of cases or is very rarely used, and that
> value is in your view not there.
> 
> >
> > 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.
> 
> I agree with this point, we should drop the model.
> 
> > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> 
> Well I made arguments on the other thread, but to repeat:
> 
> - It makes it easier to engage with people when they do ack it.
> 
> - It makes it far quicker to be able to do so.
> 
> - There's a barrier to mentioning an LLM if it's not provided - people can get
>   upset, or it can cause issues to raise it as a concern.
> 
> - Even if it's only there in _some_ cases, it makes _those_ cases easier to deal
>   with.

As far as I understand, all the above arguments would also be addressed
with either a free-formed mention of LLM usage, or a formal
"non-advertising" tag that is not merged in the kernel history, right ?

> - It provides some (incomplete) data that might make it easier to deal with
>   bug-causing patches.
> 
> - It provides some (incomplete) data on bug rates with/without LLMs.

For those two I suppose a machine-parseable tag in the git history could
improve things slightly compared to information provided in the patch
submission that would not get recorded in the history.

Is this why you have a preference for a formal tag compared to a
free-formed mention ?

> I do agree they're far far less useful when there's not some indication of how
> much of the patch was LLM-generated.
> 
> Their usefulness is obviously deeply far from perfect, but not zero.
> 
> > Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> > collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> 
> I feel there are purposes. Perhaps the argument is stronger for having the tags
> on submissions rather than actually in-tree, however.

I really think we need to have the information at submission. I think I
have a slight preference for not recording it in the kernel tree, but
only slight.

> > Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> 
> I think simplifying by requiring only 'LLM' and adding a line simply suggesting
> a comment giving a sense of how much was used, and then perhaps a paragraph saying

> 
> Could be a compromise?
>

> > Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> > it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> > of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> > reliable.

>

> > -  Assisted-by: Claude:claude-3-opus coccinelle sparse
> > diff --git a/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst b/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst
> > index cc6a1f73d7f2..b74c38aa9770 100644
> > --- a/Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:07:03PM +0200, Greg KH wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> > months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> > is quite low.
> > 
> > 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> > the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> 
> Then let's move to get people to follow it.

Has there been any discussion regarding *how* to do so ? Jon mentioned
that in many cases the failure to disclose is simply due to ignorance.
That could be addressed by more advertising (not entirely sure where
though, documentation isn't always read), or restructuring of
coding-assistants.rst (as far as I understand, the file is supposed to
be consumed by agents, so it should be reasonable to expect the LLM
output to contain a tag).

There are also cases where submitters refuse to comply with the rules
(as in [1] apparently). For those cases, a clear message that
maintainers will be fully supported by the community when they push back
could help.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260527110147.17815-1-tao.wangtao@honor.com/

> > 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.
> 
> Who cares, make up a name, all I want is the "signal" that someone is
> using a LLM so that I can review it as-such.  And if I think someone is
> not reporting that, I can ask for them to properly attribute it and if
> they lie, well, that's on them.
> 
> > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> 
> We want to know if a LLM is being used.
> 
> > Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> > collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> 
> No, please do not do this.  It's useful already for many patches in my
> subsystems, and is only going to be used more in the future.
> 
> thanks,
> 
> greg k-h
> 

-- 
Regards,

Laurent Pinchart

> But why? What do you intend to do with this information?
> 
> Do you mean to use it as an indicator that the patch should receive
> "extra" review (or maybe that it should be ignored)? Do you mean to use
> it to generate some sort of statistics at a later time? 

I use the information to decide how to review the patch, and what level
of priority to give it. For that usage I don't need a tag, but I need
the information in some human-readable form at patch submission time.

> If we want to collect some sort of structured data like this, it would
> be good to have some idea of how it will be used. That might inform the
> format and content of what we're collecting.
> 
> > > Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> > > collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> > > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> > 
> > No, please do not do this.  It's useful already for many patches in my
> > subsystems, and is only going to be used more in the future.
> 
> I should have sent this as an RFC patch, but hopefully everyone is
> treating that way anyway. To be clear, I don't have a strong preference
> on whether we keep Assisted-by: or not, but I'd like to better
> understand the intended uses of it if we do intend to keep it.

-- 
Regards,

Laurent Pinchart

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes expressed concerns that Jeff Layton's argument for removing LLM attribution requirements is flawed, as it only considers the lack of universal adherence to the rule and not its actual usage or value in facilitating engagement and data collection. He suggested a compromise where the requirement would be simplified to include only 'LLM' and a comment on the extent of AI-generated code, with an additional paragraph for subsystem-specific acceptance.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm a little surprised I'm cc'd on this :) I'm not entirely sure if my pushing
back on this is going to mean anything but I suppose here goes nothing.

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> is quite low.
>
> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.

Does something not being universally followed therefore make it worthless?

You really have to explain that, because this is literally true of any rule
whatsoever we might have in the kernel, should we drop all of them?

I think you should replace this with a cogent argument such as that you feel it
is not being used in the _majority_ of cases or is very rarely used, and that
value is in your view not there.

>
> 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.

I agree with this point, we should drop the model.

>
> 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.

Well I made arguments on the other thread, but to repeat:

- It makes it easier to engage with people when they do ack it.

- It makes it far quicker to be able to do so.

- There's a barrier to mentioning an LLM if it's not provided - people can get
  upset, or it can cause issues to raise it as a concern.

- Even if it's only there in _some_ cases, it makes _those_ cases easier to deal
  with.

- It provides some (incomplete) data that might make it easier to deal with
  bug-causing patches.

- It provides some (incomplete) data on bug rates with/without LLMs.

I do agree they're far far less useful when there's not some indication of how
much of the patch was LLM-generated.

Their usefulness is obviously deeply far from perfect, but not zero.

>
> Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.

I feel there are purposes. Perhaps the argument is stronger for having the tags
on submissions rather than actually in-tree, however.

>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

I think simplifying by requiring only 'LLM' and adding a line simply suggesting
a comment giving a sense of how much was used, and then perhaps a paragraph saying

	Subsystems may vary in their acceptance of this patch blah blah blah

Could be a compromise?

> ---
> Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> reliable.

Well he's moved now to stridently saying we should do what you're doing here so
presumably you're now in alignment.

>
> Who cares, make up a name, all I want is the "signal" that someone is
> using a LLM so that I can review it as-such.  And if I think someone is
> not reporting that, I can ask for them to properly attribute it and if
> they lie, well, that's on them.
>
> > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
>
> We want to know if a LLM is being used.

> subsystems, and is only going to be used more in the future.
>
> thanks,
>
> greg k-h

Entirely my view and experience. The tags are proactively useful, even if flawed
and (possibly very) incomplete.

I am totally fine with getting rid of the model however, and I do think it'd be
useful to add a small paragraph suggesting that people add a comment indicating
_how much_ of the patch was LLM-generated, even if it's vague and fuzzy.

Some information > no information.

Thanks, Lorenzo

> Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org> writes:
>
> > On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> >> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several

> >>
> >> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> >> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> >
> > Then let's move to get people to follow it.
>
> FWIW, in my experience, simple ignorance of the rules is one of the
> biggest reasons why so many people fail to follow them.  When I've asked
> docs contributors about the source of their work, they tend to come
> clean, apologize, and add the tags going forward.

I also feel that repeatedly establishing boundaries like this (in as civil
and reasonable way as possible) can help establish the 'done thing'.

We will probably still experience a great deal of unattributed patches, and
I think over time we'll have to figure out a way to deal with that, but
there's certainly a cohort who can be reached, and it's beneficial to do
so.

It also makes it easier to enforce with those who don't play nice - 'here
are the rules, look over here, and look at these patches' etc.

With no rules at all it becomes far harder to enforce anything.

>
> jon

Thanks, Lorenzo

Well I prefer a tag for reasons below. I'm fine with non-advertising yeah.

>
> > - It provides some (incomplete) data that might make it easier to deal with
> >   bug-causing patches.
> >
> > - It provides some (incomplete) data on bug rates with/without LLMs.

> improve things slightly compared to information provided in the patch
> submission that would not get recorded in the history.

Yeah, again I could live without it being in the tree. I think there's some
advantages though.

See below for tag argument.

>
> Is this why you have a preference for a formal tag compared to a
> free-formed mention ?
>
> > I do agree they're far far less useful when there's not some indication of how
> > much of the patch was LLM-generated.

> > > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> >

> > on submissions rather than actually in-tree, however.
>
> I really think we need to have the information at submission. I think I
> have a slight preference for not recording it in the kernel tree, but
> only slight.

It seems we are more in agreement than it seems :)

I prefer a tag as it's clear cut and easily greppable and easily noticeable.

If it's words it can be vague or be unclear or inconsistent and you might miss
etc.

So I have a strong preference for a tag, would love to add details about how
much LLM done (that'll be vague obviously + have grey lines).

I agree gettig rid of model is worthwhile but I'm not bothered if we don't do
that.

Thanks, Lorenzo

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes noted that the current tagging system for LLM attribution is flawed and has low value for real analysis due to self-reporting and a nebulous definition of what 'LLM was used' actually means, but still provides practical human value in signaling whether an LLM was used without being aggressive. He suggested using a standardized tag for this information instead of cluttering changelogs with tags.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 12:11:20PM -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
>
>
> On Thu, Jul 2, 2026, at 10:32 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> > months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> > is quite low.
> >
> > 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> > the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> >
> > 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.
> >
> > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> >
> > Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> > collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> >
> > Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> > ---
> > Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> > it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> > of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> > reliable.
>
> I agree that the current tagging system is flawed and almost useless
> for real analysis -- self-reporting and a nebulous definition of what
> "LLM was used" actually means are red flags for any data scientist.
>
> I don't have a stand on whether the tagging should be removed or fixed.
> But today I ignore it (my 2-cents US) for these reasons.

Oh no doubt they have highly dubious value as _data_.

But they are useful for practical purposes :) i.e. 'ok I can talk about the
LLM-ness here without it being quite so aggressive to do so because it's
admitted'.

And also political value in that 'here is how you are supposed to do it'
vs. <how you did it>.

I think there's definite practical human stuff that they provide sufficient for
there to be value here, and I've found that in practice also :)

(Though of course it's far far from resolving how we deal with the unattributed
stuff, which remains an ongoing issue!)

>
> --
> Chuck Lever

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

> > -
> > -Attribution
> > -===========
> > -
> > -When AI tools contribute to kernel development, proper attribution
> > -helps track the evolving role of AI in the development process.
> > -Contributions should include an Assisted-by tag in the following format::
> > -
> > -  Assisted-by: AGENT_NAME:MODEL_VERSION [TOOL1] [TOOL2]
> > -
> > -Where:
> > -
> > -* ``AGENT_NAME`` is the name of the AI tool or framework
> > -* ``MODEL_VERSION`` is the specific model version used
> > -* ``[TOOL1] [TOOL2]`` are optional specialized analysis tools used
> > -  (e.g., coccinelle, sparse, smatch, clang-tidy)
> > -
> > -Basic development tools (git, gcc, make, editors) should not be listed.
> > -
> > -Example::

> Documentation/process/generated-content.rst still clearly requests

With a link to generated-content.rst?

On Thu, 2026-07-02 at 17:07 +0200, Greg KH wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several

> > the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
> 
> Then let's move to get people to follow it.

> 
> Who cares, make up a name, all I want is the "signal" that someone is
> using a LLM so that I can review it as-such.  And if I think someone is
> not reporting that, I can ask for them to properly attribute it and if
> they lie, well, that's on them.
> 
> > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> 
> We want to know if a LLM is being used.

But why? What do you intend to do with this information?

Do you mean to use it as an indicator that the patch should receive
"extra" review (or maybe that it should be ignored)? Do you mean to use
it to generate some sort of statistics at a later time? 

If we want to collect some sort of structured data like this, it would
be good to have some idea of how it will be used. That might inform the
format and content of what we're collecting.

> > Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> > collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> > requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
> 
> No, please do not do this.  It's useful already for many patches in my
> subsystems, and is only going to be used more in the future.
> 

I should have sent this as an RFC patch, but hopefully everyone is
treating that way anyway. To be clear, I don't have a strong preference
on whether we keep Assisted-by: or not, but I'd like to better
understand the intended uses of it if we do intend to keep it.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested that instead of removing the requirement for LLM attribution, we should focus on educating contributors about the rules and encouraging them to follow it

FWIW, in my experience, simple ignorance of the rules is one of the
biggest reasons why so many people fail to follow them.  When I've asked

clean, apologize, and add the tags going forward.

jon

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current LLM attribution system is flawed due to self-reporting and a vague definition of 'LLM was used', making it nearly useless for data analysis, and suggested either removing or fixing the tagging system.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 2, 2026, at 10:32 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> is quite low.
>
> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.
>
> 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.
>
> 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
>
> Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.
>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
> Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> reliable.

I agree that the current tagging system is flawed and almost useless
for real analysis -- self-reporting and a nebulous definition of what
"LLM was used" actually means are red flags for any data scientist.

I don't have a stand on whether the tagging should be removed or fixed.
But today I ignore it (my 2-cents US) for these reasons.

-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Andreas Dilger
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested that instead of trying to educate contributors about LLM attribution, the LLM itself should be programmed to add the necessary tags when generating commit messages and/or reviewing code
review_comments.raw_body:
On Jul 2, 2026, at 09:13, Jonathan Corbet <corbet@lwn.net> wrote:
> 
> Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org> writes:
> 
>> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
>>> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
>>> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
>>> is quite low.
>>> 
>>> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
>>> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.

> FWIW, in my experience, simple ignorance of the rules is one of the
> biggest reasons why so many people fail to follow them.  When I've asked
> docs contributors about the source of their work, they tend to come
> clean, apologize, and add the tags going forward.

It would make sense IMHO to address the ignorance in the most expedient manner,
namely by telling the *LLM itself* to add this tag when it generates the commit
message and/or reviews the code.

This could be achieved by adding a statement in "AGENTS.md" in the root of the
source tree to this effect, or telling the agent to reference and follow rules
in Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst regarding the Assisted-by: tag.

Cheers, Andreas

At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is
kind of lacking:

What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?

Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:

1. I used an llm to one-shot vibe-code a patch
2. I used an llm to write a patch but carefully reviewed every line
3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
manually
4. I used an llm to debug or reproduce a kernel issue but then wrote the
fix manually after fully understanding the defect
5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote
6. I used an llm to research some chunk of code while writing a patch
7. I used Google while writing a patch and learned something valuable
from the AI overview at the top

I personally would 100% use the tag for 1 or 2, and have already done
so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically
make every patch llm-assisted to the point of the distinction being
meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)
then my mistake and I will certainly start doing so. I would hope most
people agree 6-7 and similar need no tag.

Similar questions abound if you use an llm to help with writing the
English text in the patch or emails.

I have a feeling that this ambiguity is part of the reason we aren't all
agreeing on the value of the tag?

Thanks,
Boris

> > --
> > Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> 
> Thanks, Lroenzo

> > > > > > Who cares, make up a name, all I want is the "signal" that someone is
> > > > > > using a LLM so that I can review it as-such.  And if I think someone is
> > > > > > not reporting that, I can ask for them to properly attribute it and if
> > > > > > they lie, well, that's on them.
> > > > > >
> > > > > > > 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.
> > > > > >
> > > > > > We want to know if a LLM is being used.
> > > > >
> > > > > But why? What do you intend to do with this information?
> > > > >
> > > > > Do you mean to use it as an indicator that the patch should receive
> > > > > "extra" review (or maybe that it should be ignored)? Do you mean to use
> > > > > it to generate some sort of statistics at a later time?
> > > >
> > > > I use the information to decide how to review the patch, and what level
> > > > of priority to give it. For that usage I don't need a tag, but I need
> > > > the information in some human-readable form at patch submission time.
> > >
> > > Same here.  I don't care about stats, I care about "how do I review this
> > > patch" and this gives me that signal that I need if faced with a
> > > llm-helped patch.
> > >
> > >
> >
> > Do we need a tag for this though?
> >
> > This seems like the kind of information that we would always require in
> > the cover letter of a series (or the little place in an individual
> > patch for comments that don't get merged). That would also allow you to
> > convey a lot more nuance about how it was used.
> >
> > ISTM asking people to disclose LLM usage in a cover letter would give
> > everyone what they want: Information about whether and possibly how an
> > LLM was used, and it also wouldn't clutter up the changelogs with these
> > tags.

I understand the concern is having the information in the git log.  I agree in
a level.  I wish cloning linux takes not too long time and huge traffic.  I
wish each commit message be lengthy only as much as needed.

> 
> It's much much clearer and easier to just have a standardised tag for that.
> 
> You can see that (and grep for that) immediately, vague paragraphs not so much.

I also agree having a formal tag can be convenient.  LWN's Assisted-by: tag
statistic was interesting enough to make me supporting this idea.

What about keep requesting this formal signature to the submitters, but also
formally allow (or, encourage) subsystem maintainers to remove the tags when
pull-request?  That's already allowed, to my understanding and some maintainers
are doing [1] today.   That can help preventing the git history of a subsystem
bloating or advertising priorietary companies, as much as the maintainer care.
Meanwhile, we can still get the information at review time, and also after
review from the mail archives.  It will still be handy as long as we use tools
like 'lei'.  As some maintainers are already removing the tags from the git
log, this may be anyway a better way.  Someone may interested in only their
subsystem history, but I find LLM users tend to contribute to multiple
subsystems, so this might actually matters.

It might be too clear to formally document this, though.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260701115302.29c66401@kernel.org


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

> At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is

review_comments.author: Theodore Tso
review_comments.summary: Theodore Tso noted that his workflow involves asking an LLM to modify code, but then manually reviewing and testing it before writing the commit description himself, which differs from the scenario where an LLM generates thousands of lines of code for a pull request. He emphasized that there's a significant distinction between modifying existing code with an LLM and 'vibe coding' a new OS from scratch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 12:46:19PM -0500, Andreas Dilger wrote:
> It would make sense IMHO to address the ignorance in the most expedient manner,
> namely by telling the *LLM itself* to add this tag when it generates the commit
> message and/or reviews the code.
> 
> This could be achieved by adding a statement in "AGENTS.md" in the root of the
> source tree to this effect, or telling the agent to reference and follow rules
> in Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst regarding the Assisted-by: tag.

This presupposes a particular workflow where the developer allows the
LLM to generate git commits.  I don't do that.  I'll ask the LLM to
modify the code, but then I'll generally fix it up --- very often by
hand because I can edit the sources than entering a prompt and waiting
for the LLM to figure out how to make the change.  I'll often run the
test cases myself, since if it takes 24 hours of VM time, and 2 hours
of wall clock time, I'm going to kick off the regression tests myself,
and verify the test results.

And I almost always write the commit description myself.  So when I
say, "Assisted-by" it literally is "Assisted by".  It is not "vibe
coded where the LLM generates thousands of lines of code that the
human being doesn't understand before sending ta pull request."  And
that's probably why you'll see people asserting that LLM generated
code can't possibly be accepted due to copyright reasons.  There's a
big difference between asking an LLM to modify already existing code
to add a feature, or to fix a bug, and to vibe code a new OS from
scratch.  One is far less likely to be a copyright violation than the
other.

						- Ted

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu()` function in the sunrpc module to route incoming RPC requests to a populated pool, rather than one that is currently idle. This ensures that requests are always serviced, even if there are fewer threads than pools. The patch trades off some NUMA locality for this guarantee, and uses a data_race() annotation to indicate that the read of `sp_nrthreads` is a best-effort routing hint.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer questioned the choice of commit being fixed, suggesting that it's more likely a different commit is responsible for the issue and requested an alternative fix.

Reviewer suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to handle the case where no pool has threads, as a defensive-programming approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Why that commit?  Did this ever work correctly?
It seems more likely that 
Fixes: 3262c816a3d7 ("[PATCH] knfsd: split svc_serv into pools")
is appropriate.

---

Would a WARN_ON_ONCE() be appropriate here?

I think this is a sensible defensive-programming approach.

Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>

Thnaks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the lack of notification when a service is not optimally configured, specifically when there are fewer threads than pools. The author agrees to add a pr_notice_once() in v5 to inform the user.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe a pr_notice_once()? A stack trace isn't particularly helpful
here, but it would be good to let someone know that this isn't
optimally configured. I'll add one for v5.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the patch's behavior was broken by a later commit and plans to fix it in the next version.

Author acknowledged reviewer feedback about skipping pools with no threads and explained that it's not feasible due to legitimate cases where calls are queued to threadless pools, especially during startup and shutdown.
review_comments.raw_body:
I had the LLM run this down. We're both wrong.

It briefly worked properly after 3262c816a3d7 ("split svc_serv into
pools"), but then was broken in the same series in commit bfd241600a3b
("knfsd: make rpc threads pools numa aware"). So I think we want:

Fixes: bfd241600a3b ("knfsd: make rpc threads pools numa aware")

---

This is probably not feasible, as there are cases where we legitimately
queue the call to a pool with no threads.

At startup, we create listeners and then threads only get spun up
later. If we get a RPC on the listener port during that window, the
message would fire. There's a similar window on shutdown.

It might not hurt to add a tracepoint there though.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown noted that if an admin requests zero threads on some pools during start-up, the system can still get stuck because svc_pool_for_cpu() will fall back to a pool with no threads, and requested a solution or clarification.
review_comments.raw_body:
and a comment explaining when this might happen?

The start-up window makes sense - as long as we start a thread on each
pool it should be safe.  But if we don't (if the admin request zero on
some pools) we can still get stuck.  Is there something simple we can do
about that, or does the admin get to keep both halves?

And on shutdown the threads stop before the sockets are cleaned up - but
they do get cleaned up, so all good.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes such as SVC_POOL_AUTO/GLOBAL/PERCPU/PERNODE. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes from a single global pool to one pool per NUMA node. This change simplifies the code and improves performance by providing proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The patch also removes deprecated code, including mode selection heuristics and switch statements, and updates documentation to reflect the new behavior.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that since sv_nrpools is either '1' or equal to svc_pool_map.count, it can be removed and only sv_is_pooled used for simplification and clarity in the code.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this patch is good, but there is probably room for further
simplification which is probably best left to a later patch.
In particular, ->sv_nrpools is now either '1' or svc_pool_map.count,
and these possibilities match ->sv_is_pooled.  So we only need one of
those.
I think removing ->sv_nrpools and only using ->sv_is_pooled would
simplify/clarify some of the code.

Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>

Thanks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed NeilBrown's suggestion to simplify the code by removing mode-based switch statements and instead using a pernode path. The author explained that this is not possible because sv_is_pooled cannot be derived from sv_nrpools due to the single-node case, and that converting all accesses of sv_nrpools would require creating more accessors for the svc_pool_map, which could have performance implications.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks. I took a look at that cleanup, but it's not quite the same.

  - unpooled service (lockd, NFS callback): sv_is_pooled=false, sv_nrpools=1
  - pooled service on a single-node host: sv_is_pooled=true, sv_nrpools=1 (pernode degenerates to one pool)
  - pooled service on multi-node: sv_is_pooled=true, sv_nrpools=N>1

So you can't derive sv_is_pooled from sv_nrpools because of the
single-node case.

Worse, it also means that we'd have to create more accessors for the
svc_pool_map. There are several places that access sv_nrpools that
would have to be converted to get it from the map. That may have
performance implications too since the map is accessed during receives.

So this kind of cleanup is doable, but it's a bit messier than it
sounds at first.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer NeilBrown agreed with the patch, but expressed skepticism about removing sv_nrpools and suggested keeping it due to its implication of a reference to svc_pool_map.

Reviewer NeilBrown noted that svc_pool_map is a single global variable and thus does not require dereferencing, making the patch's concern about accessing shared pool_to[] array unnecessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agree.  We have to keep sv_is_pooled (it implies a reference to
svc_pool_map).  I'm not convinced that we need sv_nrpools.

---

svc_pool_map is a single global that is read-mostly, so no dereference,
easy to predict.
During receive we need to access svc_pool_map anyway to map CPU to POOL.
So the svc_pool_map.npools will have to be pulled into cache anyway.

But if you don't like it, I won't push it on you.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that each CPU-bearing node in a NUMA system has at least one thread when distributing RPC threads automatically. When the requested number of threads is less than the number of pools, some nodes' pools would be left empty under even distribution. The patch floors each pool's thread count to 1 for CPU-bearing nodes, preventing empty pools and potential service disruptions. This change only affects automatic thread distribution and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set by callers. Nodes without CPUs are unaffected.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown noted that the condition in svc_set_num_threads() is unnecessary because svc_pool_map_init_pernode() ensures each node with a pool has at least one CPU, and suggested addressing the problem outside the loop by setting remain to serv->sv_nrpools when base is zero and nrservs is non-zero.
review_comments.raw_body:
svc_pool_map_init_pernode() uses for_each_node_with_cpus() so we can be
certain that each node which has been allocated a pool will have at
least 1 cpu.  Thus that last condition isn't needed.

I would probably address the problem outside the loop with

if (base == 0 && nrservs != 0)
   /* We need at least one thread per pool for correct functionality */
   remain = serv->sv_nrpools;

or similar.  But your version works too and this isn't
performance-critical code.

Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>

Thanks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees to use NeilBrown's suggested modification, which guarantees a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing threads.
review_comments.raw_body:
I like your version better. I'll adopt it.

Thanks!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] sunrpc: eliminate a modulus operation from the enqueueing codepath
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series eliminates a modulus operation in the sunrpc enqueueing codepath, replacing it with an explicit check to avoid array index out-of-bounds errors. The change is motivated by performance considerations, as the original modulus operation was expensive and not pipelined.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Suggested discarding ->sv_nrpools as a solution to eliminate the modulus operation. This would simplify the code and avoid the need for an explicit check.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 02 Jul 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Currently we do this to determine the pool to enqueue on:
> 
>     pidx = m->to_pool[cpu_to_node(raw_smp_processor_id())] % serv->sv_nrpools;
> 
> ...but a modulus is rather expensive. Replace this instead with an
> explicit check for running off the end of the array.
> 
> This situation should never occur, but if it does, just fall back to
> pool 0.
> 
> This trades a ~20-30 cycle operation that isn't pipelined and
> monopolizes the divider for a ~1 cycle well-predicted branch.

I would rather discard ->sv_nrpools as described in previous reply, then
this modulus would disappear as it isn't needed.

Thanks,
NeilBrown


> 
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 4 +++-
>  1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> index ae93a6f51087..ed841ea09079 100644
> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> @@ -252,7 +252,9 @@ struct svc_pool *svc_pool_for_cpu(struct svc_serv *serv)
>  	if (serv->sv_nrpools <= 1)
>  		return serv->sv_pools;
>  
> -	pidx = m->to_pool[cpu_to_node(raw_smp_processor_id())] % serv->sv_nrpools;
> +	pidx = m->to_pool[cpu_to_node(raw_smp_processor_id())];
> +	if (pidx >= serv->sv_nrpools)
> +		pidx = 0;
>  
>  	/*
>  	 * Threads are spread evenly across the pools, but when there are
> 
> -- 
> 2.54.0
> 
>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to change the code based on reviewer's suggestion, adopting their preferred approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
I like your version better. I'll adopt it.

Thanks!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown noted that if an admin requests zero threads on some pools during start-up, the system can still get stuck, and requested a solution to this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
and a comment explaining when this might happen?

The start-up window makes sense - as long as we start a thread on each
pool it should be safe.  But if we don't (if the admin request zero on
some pools) we can still get stuck.  Is there something simple we can do
about that, or does the admin get to keep both halves?

And on shutdown the threads stop before the sockets are cleaned up - but
they do get cleaned up, so all good.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer NeilBrown agreed with the patch, but expressed concern about removing sv_nrpools and suggested keeping sv_is_pooled to maintain a reference to svc_pool_map.

Reviewer NeilBrown noted that svc_pool_map is a single global variable accessed read-mostly, and suggested pulling the svc_pool_map.npools value into cache during receive operations since it's already being accessed anyway.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agree.  We have to keep sv_is_pooled (it implies a reference to
svc_pool_map).  I'm not convinced that we need sv_nrpools.

---

svc_pool_map is a single global that is read-mostly, so no dereference,
easy to predict.
During receive we need to access svc_pool_map anyway to map CPU to POOL.
So the svc_pool_map.npools will have to be pulled into cache anyway.

But if you don't like it, I won't push it on you.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] Documentation: remove the requirement for LLM attribution
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the requirement for attributing Large Language Model (LLM) assistance in kernel documentation, aiming to clarify the distinction between meaningful and trivial LLM contributions.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: reviewer prefers standardized 'Reviewed-by' tags over LLM attribution in changelogs and suggests using a cover letter to convey more nuanced information
review_comments.raw_body:
It's much much clearer and easier to just have a standardised tag for that.

You can see that (and grep for that) immediately, vague paragraphs not so much.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov questioned the threshold for using an 'Assisted-by: LLM' tag, citing ambiguity in levels of assistance and suggesting that only cases where a patch was written entirely by an LLM or reviewed by one should be tagged.
review_comments.raw_body:
At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is
kind of lacking:

What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?

Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:

1. I used an llm to one-shot vibe-code a patch
2. I used an llm to write a patch but carefully reviewed every line
3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
manually
4. I used an llm to debug or reproduce a kernel issue but then wrote the
fix manually after fully understanding the defect
5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote
6. I used an llm to research some chunk of code while writing a patch
7. I used Google while writing a patch and learned something valuable
from the AI overview at the top

I personally would 100% use the tag for 1 or 2, and have already done
so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically
make every patch llm-assisted to the point of the distinction being
meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)
then my mistake and I will certainly start doing so. I would hope most
people agree 6-7 and similar need no tag.

Similar questions abound if you use an llm to help with writing the
English text in the patch or emails.

I have a feeling that this ambiguity is part of the reason we aren't all
agreeing on the value of the tag?

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: SJ Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park agrees that the information in the git log is a concern, but wishes for shorter clone times and less traffic instead of addressing the issue directly.

Reviewer suggested that instead of requiring a formal tag for LLM attribution, the patch should request submitters to include this information in their cover letter and allow subsystem maintainers to remove these tags from the pull request, which would help prevent git history bloating.
review_comments.raw_body:
I understand the concern is having the information in the git log.  I agree in
a level.  I wish cloning linux takes not too long time and huge traffic.  I
wish each commit message be lengthy only as much as needed.

---

I also agree having a formal tag can be convenient.  LWN's Assisted-by: tag
statistic was interesting enough to make me supporting this idea.

What about keep requesting this formal signature to the submitters, but also
formally allow (or, encourage) subsystem maintainers to remove the tags when
pull-request?  That's already allowed, to my understanding and some maintainers
are doing [1] today.   That can help preventing the git history of a subsystem
bloating or advertising priorietary companies, as much as the maintainer care.
Meanwhile, we can still get the information at review time, and also after
review from the mail archives.  It will still be handy as long as we use tools
like 'lei'.  As some maintainers are already removing the tags from the git
log, this may be anyway a better way.  Someone may interested in only their
subsystem history, but I find LLM users tend to contribute to multiple
subsystems, so this might actually matters.

It might be too clear to formally document this, though.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260701115302.29c66401@kernel.org


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: SJ Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park noted that the 'Assisted-by: LLM' tag should only be used when the user has completely reviewed and understands the text, and suggested that patches without this level of review should not be submitted even if tagged.

Reviewer expressed skepticism about the difficulty of defining what constitutes 'Assisted-by: LLM' attribution, citing a series of hypothetical scenarios to illustrate the ambiguity
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this doesn't need the tag, too.  Like patches, the user should
completely reviewed and understand the text, though.  Without the complete
review, it should not be submmitted at all, regardless of the tag.

---

I'm not very sure...  I don't really feel the question is that difficult and
ambiguous to answer.  I may be biased.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Laurent Pinchart
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Laurent Pinchart uses the LLM attribution information to determine review priority and level of scrutiny, requesting that this data be available in a human-readable format at patch submission time
review_comments.raw_body:
I use the information to decide how to review the patch, and what level
of priority to give it. For that usage I don't need a tag, but I need
the information in some human-readable form at patch submission time.

review_comments.author: Greg KH
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Greg KH expressed concern about the potential removal of Assisted-by: attribution, citing its importance for reviewers to identify patches that have received assistance from LLM tools.
review_comments.raw_body:
Same here.  I don't care about stats, I care about "how do I review this
patch" and this gives me that signal that I need if faced with a
llm-helped patch.

So it needs to stay please.

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Laurent Pinchart
review_comments.summary: reviewer questioned whether replacing 'Assisted-by: <product name>' with 'Assisted-by: LLM' would adequately address the free advertising issue, and requested an acceptable alternative
review_comments.raw_body:
There's the free advertising issue we still need to address. Would the
proposed "Assisted-by: LLM" tag to replace product names be an
acceptable option for you ?

-- 
Regards,

Laurent Pinchart

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to Greg KH's feedback about collecting structured data on LLM usage, explaining that a cover letter or comments section would be a better place for this information and could convey more nuance.

This seems like the kind of information that we would always require in

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 3/4] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing
Re: [PATCH v4 2/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to per-node in the sunrpc code, effectively removing other modes. The change is necessary because it's not possible to derive whether a service is pooled or not based on the number of pools alone due to edge cases like single-node hosts. This cleanup simplifies the code and avoids potential performance implications from accessing the svc_pool_map during receives.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary:
NeilBrown noted that deriving sv_is_pooled from sv_nrpools is problematic due to the single-node case, and suggested removing sv_nrpools altogether since it implies a reference to svc_pool_map

Reviewer pointed out that deriving sv_is_pooled from sv_nrpools is not possible due to the single-node case, and suggested that pulling svc_pool_map.npools into cache during receive would be acceptable
review_comments.raw_body:
Agree.  We have to keep sv_is_pooled (it implies a reference to
svc_pool_map).  I'm not convinced that we need sv_nrpools.

---

svc_pool_map is a single global that is read-mostly, so no dereference,
easy to predict.
During receive we need to access svc_pool_map anyway to map CPU to POOL.
So the svc_pool_map.npools will have to be pulled into cache anyway.

But if you don't like it, I won't push it on you.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that the patch needs a fix and plans to add a pr_notice_once() in v5, acknowledging that the current configuration is not optimal.

Author acknowledged reviewer feedback about potential RPC queueing issues during startup and shutdown, explained that it is legitimate in certain cases, but suggested adding a tracepoint for debugging purposes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I had the LLM run this down. We're both wrong.

It briefly worked properly after 3262c816a3d7 ("split svc_serv into
pools"), but then was broken in the same series in commit bfd241600a3b
("knfsd: make rpc threads pools numa aware"). So I think we want:

Fixes: bfd241600a3b ("knfsd: make rpc threads pools numa aware")

---

This is probably not feasible, as there are cases where we legitimately
queue the call to a pool with no threads.

At startup, we create listeners and then threads only get spun up
later. If we get a RPC on the listener port during that window, the
message would fire. There's a similar window on shutdown.

It might not hurt to add a tracepoint there though.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that if admin requests zero threads on some pools, it can lead to a situation where the system gets stuck, and requested a simple solution or alternative configuration option
review_comments.raw_body:
and a comment explaining when this might happen?

The start-up window makes sense - as long as we start a thread on each
pool it should be safe.  But if we don't (if the admin request zero on
some pools) we can still get stuck.  Is there something simple we can do
about that, or does the admin get to keep both halves?

And on shutdown the threads stop before the sockets are cleaned up - but
they do get cleaned up, so all good.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] docs: link AI coding assistants and tool-generated content pages

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 00/18] iomap: convert to in-iter iomap_next() model

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 00/18] iomap: convert to in-iter iomap_next() model

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/18] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new callback function called ->iomap_next() that replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() functions. The ->iomap_next() function is responsible for finishing the previous mapping if needed and producing the next mapping, allowing performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to handle most of the logic required in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change allows filesystems to be converted one at a time using the new ->iomap_next() function.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch introduces an overly long line, but considers it a minor issue as it will be resolved by the end of the series.

Reviewer suggested inlining code from iomap_iter_next() into this function and replacing the legacy call with a conditional check for the presence of ->iomap_next().

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig requested a prep patch to factor out iomap_iter() logic before refactoring it into ->iomap_next() and iomap_process(), suggesting that this change should be done in a way that minimizes git blame disturbance by keeping the code in roughly the same place.
sentiment_signals: resolved by the end of the series
review_comments.raw_body:
Overly long line here.  Not that it really matters too much given
that this goes away by the end of the series.

---

Maybe inline the code currently in iomap_iter_next here from the
start, as that what we're getting to by the end of the series anyway.
And just do a:

	if (!ops->iomap_next))
		return iomap_iter_legacy(iter, ops);

at the beginning?

---

It seems like this is largely just factored out form the old iomap_iter().
Can you do a prep patch before this one which factors this out?
Preferably in a way that keeps the code in roughly the same place to
minimize the git blame disturbance?

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the compiler cannot directly call ->iomap_next() even after inlining iomap_process(), and requested a comment to clarify the importance of iomap_process() being an inline function.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the iomap_iter_next() function and its callers can take non-const pointers to struct iomap_ops, which is unnecessary since the ops structure does not contain any mutable state.
review_comments.raw_body:
Er... what is being called directly here?  The iomap_{begin,end}
functions?  It looks to me like ->iomap_next is still an indirect call
even at the end of the series, right?  That's still a net reduction of
indirect calls at least.

Oh, wait, it's the __always_inline iomap_process function that a smart
compiler can use to turn the indirect calls into direct ones, isn't it?
It might be useful to add a comment to that function saying that out
loud so that dolts like me will pick up on why it's critical for it to
be an inline function.

I like how this is going. :)

---

These both could be const, right?

--D

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that a sentence in the patch description was confusingly worded, agreed to add a clarifying comment and reword the commit message.

Author agreed to address a minor issue by marking the iomap_ops structure members as const, confirming that no further revision is needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ahh the sentence was confusingly / ambiguously worded, sorry about
that - I was trying to say that it lets callers have the ability to
inline iomap_iter() with passing a const iomap_next callback as an arg
which lets the iomap_next callback be a plain direct call. But your
interpretation of it is also true. I'll add the comment you suggested
to spell what you mentioned out and reword the commit message to make
what I was trying to say more clear.

---

I'll mark these as const. Thanks for looking at these patches!

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to restructure the code by extracting shared logic into a preparatory patch in response to reviewer feedback about code organization.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sounds good, I'll extract this shared logic out into a preparatory patch.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/18] xfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for XFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient and flexible way of handling I/O mappings. The conversion uses the iomap_process() helper function to finish previous mappings and produce the next one. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather updates the internal implementation of XFS's iomap operations. The patch affects several files in the fs/xfs directory, including xfs_file.c and xfs_iomap.c.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested dropping the arrow notation in the iomap_next method to avoid potential confusion when the ops struct is removed

Reviewer noted that the patch could benefit from a simplified version of iomap_process, potentially reducing complexity and improving readability, but expressed uncertainty about its effectiveness.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: Maybe the drop the "->" in preparation of the last patch that would
make it slightly misleading with the ops struct removal?

---

I wonder if we want a "simple" version of iomap_process that only takes
a begin callback, as most current ops seems to be that way.  Then again
between needing at least one longer function name it's probably not
helping much with readability.  So not sure, just dropping it here if
anyone has a good idea or storng opinion.

Otherwise looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested bikeshedding the name of the ->iomap_next() function, questioning its purpose and whether it should be named after looking up the first mapping, releasing resources to look for the next mapping, or deciding when to stop iterating.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested renaming xfs_direct_write_iomap_next() to xfs_direct_write_iomap_iter(), citing that it would better describe the function's purpose of iterating iomaps for direct writes.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the introduction of 'iomap_iter' creates a naming conflict and suggested renaming the iomap_process function to something like 'iomap_iter_yield', but acknowledged the difficulty in choosing a suitable name due to C's lack of Python-like naming conventions.
review_comments.raw_body:
<unvarnished brainstream ahead>

Now that I see the callsites, I think the "next" name could use some
bikeshedding <cough>.  The purpose of this function is either

(a) to look up the first mapping to start iterating;
(b) to release whatever resources were attached during the current
    iteration and look up the next mapping to continue iterating; or
(c) to decide that it's time to stop iterating.

---

iomaps for iteration.  In Python those are called generator functions;
in Rust they're called objects that implement the Iterator trait (or
iterators for short).

How about s/iomap_next/iomap_iter/ ?

Then this function would be called xfs_direct_write_iomap_iter, which
IMO is a closer description of what the function does, which is to say
iterates iomaps for direct writes.

(Yes, my brain might be polluted with thinking that "iomap next" refers
to a major shift, in the sense of "linux next". :P)

---

But then "iomap_iter" sets up a new problem: should iomap_process have a
new name that goes along with that?  iomap_iter is already taken for the
legacy path.  If we were emulating python I'd suggest iomap_iter_yield
but this is C so we get to reinvent everything from scratch so who
knows.

--D

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested using a macro to simulate a default NULL iomap_end_fn parameter in the iomap_process() helper, instead of relying on a bunch of fugly macros.
review_comments.raw_body:
You /could/ use a bunch of fugly macros to simulate a default NULL
iomap_end_fn parameter.

#define __default_iomap_end(a,b,...) b
#define default_iomap_end(...) __default_iomap_end(,##__VA_ARGS,NULL)

#define iomap_process(iter, iomap, srcmap, iomap_begin, ...) \
	__iomap_process((iter), (iomap), (srcmap), (iomap_begin),
			default_iomap_end(__VA_ARGS__))

--D

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Joanne Koong is responding to feedback about naming conventions in the patch, specifically whether the _next suffix for callback functions could be confusing given existing iter-related names in iomap.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for sharing your thoughts. The idea makes sense to me in theory
but I think in rust and python the "iter" naming is reserved for the
iterator object itself and the method / callback for advancing and
yielding the next item in the iteration is named "next" (eg
Iterator::next in rust and __next__() in python)? I wonder if the
s/_next/_iter gets confusing with multiple things in iomap already
called iter (eg the struct iomap_iter, the iomap_iter() function). I'm
not sure, I'm happy to rename this if that's the preference.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the naming of the iomap_next() callback could be improved to explicitly convey its per-iteration logic, agreed to rename it in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ahh okay, I think I see the point you were trying to make. The
callsite functions are like:

ssize_t iomap_file_buffered_write(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from,
                iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                const struct iomap_write_ops *write_ops, void *private);

int iomap_fsverity_write(struct file *file, loff_t pos, size_t length,
                const void *buf, iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                const struct iomap_write_ops *write_ops);

void iomap_read_folio(iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                struct iomap_read_folio_ctx *ctx, void *private);

void iomap_readahead(iomap_next_fn iomap_next,
                struct iomap_read_folio_ctx *ctx, void *private);

etc, so it's not clear from the "iomap_next_fn iomap_next" naming that
it handles per-iteration logic, whereas renaming it to "iomap_iter_fn
iomap_iter" makes it more explicit. Am I interpreting this correctly?

I think this is a good idea. If no one has an objection, I'll make
this change for v3.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the new iomap_next() callback should be named consistently, as most existing functions follow a 'next' naming convention.

Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned whether the change to ->iomap_next() is sufficient, suggesting that a more substantial change might be needed (specifically mentioning iomap_yield())
review_comments.raw_body:
That's a good point that the functions themselves are usually some
variant of next.

---

Any thoughts on iomap_yield()?  Though I can live with iomap_next since
that /is/ the least effort action. :)

--D

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that xfs_direct_write_iomap_next() and other similar functions do not check if iomaps are already being processed before calling iomap_process(), which could lead to infinite recursion.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok by me.  Ignore the email I just sent, please :)

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/18] btrfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the Btrfs direct-io code to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a helper function that finishes previous mappings and produces the next one. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather adapts the existing code to the updated iomap API. The conversion involves updating the btrfs_dio_iomap_ops structure to point to the new ->iomap_next() callback.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/18] ntfs3: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the ntfs3 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient way of handling iomap operations. The update uses the iomap_process() helper function to finish previous mappings and produce the next one. No functional changes are intended by this conversion.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/18] ntfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the NTFS file system's iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback. The existing iomap_begin and iomap_end callbacks are replaced with a single iomap_next callback that uses the iomap_process helper function. This change does not introduce any functional changes, but rather adapts the NTFS code to the new iomap API. The patch updates several functions in fs/ntfs/iomap.c to use the new iomap_next callback, including ntfs_read_iomap_ops, ntfs_zero_read_iomap_ops, and ntfs_write_iomap_ops.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/18] ext4: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series converts ext4 iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which simplifies and streamlines the mapping process by reusing existing helpers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/18] Documentation: iomap: update docs to reflect iomap_next model
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect the new iomap_next model, where every iomap operation takes a single iomap_next callback directly. The callback finishes the previous mapping and produces the next one. Most filesystems build it from begin and end helpers via the iomap_process helper. This change replaces the separate ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks used in older versions of iomap.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned the assumption that filesystems will always use iomap_process(), asking for clarification on when they might not

Reviewer Darrick Wong pointed out that the documentation for iomap_end should be updated to reflect that it must persist data for write operations, specifically mentioning that iomap_writepages does not handle inline data.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation for iomap_next() does not accurately describe its behavior, specifically that it is responsible for finishing the previous mapping in addition to producing the next one.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested adding a transition sentence to clarify that filesystems should call iomap_process instead of implementing all behaviors in ->iomap_next themselves, and also proposed demoting the next section for better clarity.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation update looks good and is fine as it is, indicating no issues or requested changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I wonder, under what circumstances would a filesystem /not/ use
iomap_process()?

---

Unrelated to this patch, but this should say "For write operations, the
end function must persist the data" because iomap_writepages doesn't
handle inline data.

---

Each call?  Oh, each implementation of ->iomap_next must finish the
previous mapping.

---

s/and/or/

I think there should be a transition sentence here along the lines of

"Most filesystems are not expected to implement all of these behaviors
in ->iomap_next themselves.  They should instead call iomap_process as
described below."

Or demote the next section so it's more obvious that the "iomap_process"
and "->iomap_next" sections aren't independent?

---

<nod> Looks fine.

--D
